testcase.name: testAppThread(AppThread, AppThread, AppThread)
testcase.system-out: PART I PART I PART I IT WAS A PLEASURE TO BURN IT WAS A PLEASURE TO BURN IT was a special pleasure to see things eaten, to see things blackened and changed. With the IT was a special pleasure to see things eaten, to see things blackened and changed. With the IT WAS A PLEASURE TO BURN brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its venomous kerosene upon the world, brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its venomous kerosene upon the world, the blood pounded in his head, and his hands were the hands of some amazing conductor playing the blood pounded in his head, and his hands were the hands of some amazing conductor playing IT was a special pleasure to see things eaten, to see things blackened and changed. With the all the symphonies of blazing and burning to bring down the tatters and charcoal ruins of history. all the symphonies of blazing and burning to bring down the tatters and charcoal ruins of history. With his symbolic helmet numbered 451 on his stolid head, and his eyes all orange flame with With his symbolic helmet numbered 451 on his stolid head, and his eyes all orange flame with brass nozzle in his fists, with this great python spitting its venomous kerosene upon the world, the thought of what came next, he flicked the igniter and the house jumped up in a gorging fire the thought of what came next, he flicked the igniter and the house jumped up in a gorging fire that burned the evening sky red and yellow and black. He strode in a swarm of fireflies. He that burned the evening sky red and yellow and black. He strode in a swarm of fireflies. He the blood pounded in his head, and his hands were the hands of some amazing conductor playing wanted above all, like the old joke, to shove a marshmallow on a stick in the furnace, while the wanted above all, like the old joke, to shove a marshmallow on a stick in the furnace, while the flapping pigeon-winged books died on the porch and lawn of the house. While the books went up flapping pigeon-winged books died on the porch and lawn of the house. While the books went up in sparkling whirls and blew away on a wind turned dark with burning. in sparkling whirls and blew away on a wind turned dark with burning. all the symphonies of blazing and burning to bring down the tatters and charcoal ruins of history. Montag grinned the fierce grin of all men singed and driven back by flame. Montag grinned the fierce grin of all men singed and driven back by flame. He knew that when he returned to the firehouse, he might wink at himself, a minstrel man, burnt- He knew that when he returned to the firehouse, he might wink at himself, a minstrel man, burnt- With his symbolic helmet numbered 451 on his stolid head, and his eyes all orange flame with corked, in the mirror. Later, going to sleep, he would feel the fiery smile still gripped by his face corked, in the mirror. Later, going to sleep, he would feel the fiery smile still gripped by his face muscles, in the dark. It never went away, that. smile, it never ever went away, as long as he muscles, in the dark. It never went away, that. smile, it never ever went away, as long as he remembered. remembered. the thought of what came next, he flicked the igniter and the house jumped up in a gorging fire He hung up his black-beetle-coloured helmet and shined it, he hung his flameproof jacket neatly; He hung up his black-beetle-coloured helmet and shined it, he hung his flameproof jacket neatly; he showered luxuriously, and then, whistling, hands in pockets, walked across the upper floor of he showered luxuriously, and then, whistling, hands in pockets, walked across the upper floor of the fire station and fell down the hole. At the last moment, when disaster seemed positive, he the fire station and fell down the hole. At the last moment, when disaster seemed positive, he that burned the evening sky red and yellow and black. He strode in a swarm of fireflies. He pulled his hands from his pockets and broke his fall by grasping the golden pole. He slid to a pulled his hands from his pockets and broke his fall by grasping the golden pole. He slid to a squeaking halt, the heels one inch from the concrete floor downstairs. squeaking halt, the heels one inch from the concrete floor downstairs. wanted above all, like the old joke, to shove a marshmallow on a stick in the furnace, while the He walked out of the fire station and along the midnight street toward the subway where the He walked out of the fire station and along the midnight street toward the subway where the silent, air-propelled train slid soundlessly down its lubricated flue in the earth and let him out silent, air-propelled train slid soundlessly down its lubricated flue in the earth and let him out flapping pigeon-winged books died on the porch and lawn of the house. While the books went up with a great puff of warm air an to the cream-tiled escalator rising to the suburb. with a great puff of warm air an to the cream-tiled escalator rising to the suburb. Whistling, he let the escalator waft him into the still night air. He walked toward the comer, Whistling, he let the escalator waft him into the still night air. He walked toward the comer, in sparkling whirls and blew away on a wind turned dark with burning. thinking little at all about nothing in particular. Before he reached the corner, however, he thinking little at all about nothing in particular. Before he reached the corner, however, he slowed as if a wind had sprung up from nowhere, as if someone had called his name. slowed as if a wind had sprung up from nowhere, as if someone had called his name. Montag grinned the fierce grin of all men singed and driven back by flame. The last few nights he had had the most uncertain feelings about the sidewalk just around the The last few nights he had had the most uncertain feelings about the sidewalk just around the He knew that when he returned to the firehouse, he might wink at himself, a minstrel man, burnt- corked, in the mirror. Later, going to sleep, he would feel the fiery smile still gripped by his face corner here, moving in the starlight toward his house. He had felt that a moment before his making the turn, someone had been there. The air seemed charged with a special calm as if muscles, in the dark. It never went away, that. smile, it never ever went away, as long as he corner here, moving in the starlight toward his house. He had felt that a moment before his remembered. someone had waited there, quietly, and only a moment before he came, simply turned to a He hung up his black-beetle-coloured helmet and shined it, he hung his flameproof jacket neatly; shadow and let him through. Perhaps his nose detected a faint perfume, perhaps the skin on the making the turn, someone had been there. The air seemed charged with a special calm as if he showered luxuriously, and then, whistling, hands in pockets, walked across the upper floor of backs of his hands, on his face, felt the temperature rise at this one spot where a person's the fire station and fell down the hole. At the last moment, when disaster seemed positive, he standing might raise the immediate atmosphere ten degrees for an instant someone had waited there, quietly, and only a moment before he came, simply turned to a pulled his hands from his pockets and broke his fall by grasping the golden pole. He slid to a squeaking halt, the heels one inch from the concrete floor downstairs. He walked out of the fire station and along the midnight street toward the subway where the shadow and let him through. Perhaps his nose detected a faint perfume, perhaps the skin on the silent, air-propelled train slid soundlessly down its lubricated flue in the earth and let him out with a great puff of warm air an to the cream-tiled escalator rising to the suburb. backs of his hands, on his face, felt the temperature rise at this one spot where a person's Whistling, he let the escalator waft him into the still night air. He walked toward the comer, standing might raise the immediate atmosphere ten degrees for an instant thinking little at all about nothing in particular. Before he reached the corner, however, he slowed as if a wind had sprung up from nowhere, as if someone had called his name. The last few nights he had had the most uncertain feelings about the sidewalk just around the corner here, moving in the starlight toward his house. He had felt that a moment before his making the turn, someone had been there. The air seemed charged with a special calm as if someone had waited there, quietly, and only a moment before he came, simply turned to a shadow and let him through. Perhaps his nose detected a faint perfume, perhaps the skin on the backs of his hands, on his face, felt the temperature rise at this one spot where a person's standing might raise the immediate atmosphere ten degrees for an instant
